channel.title: DutchNews.nl
channel.description: DutchNews.nl brings daily news from The Netherlands in English

item.title: Ex-police officers seek recognition after apology to Moluccans
item.description:
Ex-police officers seek recognition after apology to Moluccans

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

A group of around 100 former police officers have asked for official recognition of what they went through during the violent confrontations with young Moluccans in the 1970s, a month after prime minister Rob Jetten apologised on behalf of the government for the treatment of the Moluccan community.

Four retired officers filed a petition with Assen city council and the police recognition foundation on behalf of the group, the Volkskrant reported. They argue that the government ordered them to enforce a hardline policy against the Moluccan community which made the situation worse, and which the state has now admitted was wrong.

“We want it recognised that the hard policy also caused suffering for the police,” retired officer Ed Jissink (76) told the paper, describing a period in which officers’ homes were firebombed and a patrol car was riddled with bullets in an ambush. Some colleagues later developed PTSD or took their own lives, he told current affairs programme Nieuwsuur.

Broken promises 
Some 12,500 Moluccans – around 3,500 soldiers who had fought on the Dutch side in the Indonesian war of independence, together with their families – were brought to the Netherlands in 1951 and housed in camps, including the former concentration camps Westerbork and Vught, on the promise that their stay would be temporary.

When it became clear the Netherlands would not support an independent Moluccan republic, frustration among the second generation boiled over into violence, including the 1977 train hijacking at De Punt, in which two hostages and six hijackers died, and the 1978 hostage-taking at the provincial government building in Assen, which left two people dead.

Police recognition 
Jissink said his own view began to change in 1974, when he saw how stateless Moluccans were treated. He later refused to take part in a training exercise practising live fire on demonstrators, which the police subsequently ended. He hopes the lesson will be remembered now that many politicians are again calling for tougher treatment of some migrant groups.

John Wattilete, president of the Moluccan government in exile, said the officers were right to raise the issue “without diminishing Moluccan suffering”, since they too had been used to manage what was really a political problem.

He is calling for a full investigation into the government’s role in the period. The national police force said the officers’ request deserved a serious response.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Picnic makes first operating profit in NL
item.description:
Picnic makes first operating profit in NL

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Online supermarket Picnic made a profit in the Netherlands for the first time last year, 10 years after its launch, according to annual figures published on Monday and reported by the Financieele Dagblad.

The Dutch arm booked an underlying operating profit of €4 million, but heavy spending on new distribution centres in Germany pushed the group as a whole to a net loss of €272 million, slightly more than the year before.

The number of customers across the Netherlands, Germany and France grew by 700,000 to 3.6 million.

Picnic, which delivers in three-quarters of the Netherlands, is still in dispute with unions FNV and CNV over which collective labour agreement should cover its delivery workers. It lost a court case last year over unpaid evening and weekend supplements and is negotiating a settlement while appealing to the Supreme Court.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Asylum agency claims €185m from hotel reception middleman
item.description:
Asylum agency claims €185m from hotel reception middleman

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The refugee settlement agency COA is claiming €184.7 million from René Derksen and his company LCHD, which arranged emergency accommodation for asylum seekers in hotels across the Netherlands, the Financieele Dagblad reported on Monday.

The agency has secured a claim against Derksen’s farmhouse in Didam, Gelderland, blocking any sale of the property. It is is one of the largest claims ever brought by a Dutch government body against a company.

COA confirmed to the paper it has started legal proceedings against LCHD and its directors over “a dispute about the execution of the assignment by LCHD and the termination of rental agreements”, but declined to comment further while the case is before the courts.

LCHD acted as COA’s exclusive intermediary for hotel accommodation from spring 2022, when the arrival of refugees from Ukraine left the agency scrambling for beds.

Over two and a half years, COA paid the company more than €500 million to book hotel rooms nationwide. FD reported earlier that Derksen paid himself more than €40 million in dividends over the period.

Derksen has been a suspect in a criminal investigation since 2023, alongside his former business partner Bob van der Valk, a member of the hotel family.

Prosecutors and the fiscal investigation service FIOD are looking into the two men’s cooperation and the money flows between them; both are suspected of, among other things, private-sector corruption.

Derksen has denied any wrongdoing, saying his prices were transparent and everyone involved knew LCHD was a commercial party making a “logical minimum margin”. LCHD has lodged a counter-claim against COA for what it calls unjustified breaches of contract.

The agency spent €2.5 billion on emergency accommodation in hotels and on cruise ships in 2024, against €1 billion on regular reception.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Major transport disruptions over A27 bridge safety warnings
item.description:
Major transport disruptions over A27 bridge safety warnings

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The Merwedebrug, which carries the A27 motorway over the river Merwede near Gorinchem, has been closed to all lorries since Saturday evening.

Roads agency Rijkswaterstaat said inspections found the bridge’s steel was weaker than expected in several places, and that it could no longer guarantee the structure would hold under heavy traffic.

Cars, vans, motorbikes, buses and emergency vehicles can still use it.

Lorries are being sent on long long detours as a result. It is not clear how long the closure will last.

Transport association TLN, which represents Dutch hauliers, said the impact was enormous. “Transport operators are already facing sharply rising costs,” the group said, pointing to a new lorry levy introduced on July 1 and higher diesel prices. “Extra detour kilometres only increase the financial pressure.”

The nearby Papendrechtsebrug closed last week for nine months of maintenance, cutting off one alternative route. The Merwedebrug was last shut to lorries in 2016, when cracks were found in its beams. That closure lasted nearly three months. A replacement bridge is due to be built starting this summer.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Drugs lab find forces evacuations in The Hague
item.description:
Drugs lab find forces evacuations in The Hague

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Up to 200 residents of a street in The Hague were allowed back into their homes at 11pm on Saturday, after police finished dismantling a drugs lab found in a house in the Segbroek district.

Residents of dozens of homes on the Meidoornstraat, along with their pets, were told to leave by 1pm on Saturday and were taken in at the district office on Fahrenheitstraat while the police and and a specialist firm removed the remaining chemicals.

The city council said on Sunday there was no longer any acute danger, and that the evacuation had been a precaution against exposure while the material was being taken away. Residents have been advised to keep their windows shut because of the smell.

Police were called to the house at around 1pm on Friday after a report of a strange smell, and found a lab still in operation. Officers shut down the production process by pumping out the chemicals. A 39-year-old man from The Hague who had come into contact with the liquids was taken to hospital and arrested.

A 34-year-old man from Voorburg was arrested at around 10pm on Friday. A 44-year-old from The Hague and a 71-year-old from Leiden were also held. Police are still investigating what type of lab it was and are canvassing the neighbourhood for witnesses and camera footage.

According to NOS, barrels holding thousands of litres of chemicals were found at the property, of which 500 to 750 litres were removed on Friday. A spokesman for the regional safety board told the broadcaster that a lab of that size would not normally be found in a residential area.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Music colleges draw internationals as Dutch student share falls
item.description:
Music colleges draw internationals as Dutch student share falls

July 20, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Only around two in 10 students training as performing musicians in the Netherlands are Dutch, according to figures from education agency DUO obtained by public broadcaster NOS.

The Dutch share of students at conservatoires – the country’s higher music education colleges – has fallen from 52% to 22% over the past 20 years. Nine HBO colleges teach music at degree level and seven offer a master’s in performance. Of those master’s students, 78% come from abroad.

People in the sector blame 15 years of cuts to music teaching. “Far from all schools have structural music education, where children can get to know music,” said Jan van den Eijnden, senior adviser at cultural education body LKCA and a teacher at the Tilburg conservatoire. Music lessons outside school have become fragmented, with big differences in cost and availability, he said.

The arts council Raad voor Cultuur has calculated that the culture sector, which includes music education, has lost more than €500 million a year relative to other sectors since 2005.

Many music schools and arts centres have closed, and there is less room for one-to-one tuition. “That comes at the cost of developing talent,” Van den Eijnden said.

The drop is showing up in amateur music too. At the Wereld Muziek Concours in Kerkrade, a four-yearly wind music festival with orchestras from 28 countries, no Dutch orchestra entered the youth division this year.

Van den Eijnden and WMC artistic director Björn Bus both said the government should guarantee organised, affordable music tuition, pointing to Belgium, where lessons are far cheaper, and Luxembourg, where free tuition is written into law.

International student numbers in the Netherlands fell for the first time in two decades this academic year, a decline the government has sought as it works to limit recruitment.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Save yourselves while you still can: an assimilated introduction
item.description:
Save yourselves while you still can: an assimilated introduction

July 19, 2026 Molly Quell

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Our regular columnist Molly Quell is worried that she might have gone a bit too far with her inburgering process. 

“They were fine, but…” I trailed off. I couldn’t quite put my finger on why I hadn’t enjoyed meeting my husband’s colleagues after we ran into them at a pub.

My (Dutch) husband and I were out with some (Dutch) friends when several of his (non-Dutch) work acquaintances happened by the bar where we were set up with beers and bitterballen.

The group was welcomed to join us, chairs were procured from nearby tables and another round of trips was ordered.

The colleagues were jolly, with conversation running from summer holiday plans to obscure folk traditions from their various homelands.

And yet, they irked me.

As he and I walked home, my husband asked the standard, post-social-engagement debrief questions. He probed about my hesitation over his colleagues.

“They didn’t introduce themselves,” I said, finally articulating what had bugged me.

The internationals, I realised, hadn’t engaged in the Dutch ritual of introducing themselves to everyone at the table, with a firm handshake and requisite eye contact.

Ten years ago, I would have told you with great confidence that this was a silly and awkward social interaction. That it is disruptive at parties and gatherings. That you forget everyone’s name immediately and then run the risk of not knowing their name if you get into real conversation later.

I’m not here to defend the practice. But I have now become so accustomed to it that it bothers me when it’s not done.

I’ve gone too far with my integration.

I’ve integrated a lot. I swim in canals. I happily go to the nude, mixed-gendered sauna. I’ve sold stuff on King’s Day and bought plates with Albert Heijn zegels.

Those are all defensible. Jumping in a canal is really keeping me cool this summer and I still use my plates.

But mandatory group introductions at social gatherings? I should self-deport and spend some time thinking about what I’ve become.

This is more or less how all human traditions come to pass. Enough people do something enough times, it becomes normalised, and now I’m congratulating 25 people sitting in a circle on someone else’s birthday.

In general I do like Dutch Introduction Culture. Dutch teenagers are much more comfortable interacting with adults than teenagers of other nationalities, in my experience. I assume this is because they have been forced from a young age to introduce themselves to everyone.

You say hello. You acknowledge that there is another person present. You do it in all facets of human interaction. At meetings and events and, goddammit, when you run into your colleague at the pub.

But to go so far as to look down upon lovely people who have no idea it is expected of them and therefore don’t do it? Next thing you know, I’ll be carrying a “Grenzen dicht” poster at a far-right rally.

When you live amongst people, you absorb their cultures and customs. I spend most of my time around Dutch people these days and my sense of reality has been warped.

Honestly I think it’s too late for me. I’m offended by the idea of putting on a bathing suit to go to a sauna. I grow increasingly frustrated with colleagues who cannot simply say directly what they want. A few weeks ago, I teased a friend for having a “luxurious” lunch.

Reader, it was a salad.

Save yourselves, get out while you still can. But make sure you firmly introduce yourself upon arrival.
item.dc:creator: Molly Quell

item.title: Dutch firms used password manager with hidden Russian roots
item.description:
Dutch firms used password manager with hidden Russian roots

July 17, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

A password manager used by several Dutch organisations concealed its Russian origins, according to an investigation by OCCRP, the international journalists’ collective, with Dutch partners Investico, De Groene Amsterdammer and NU.nl.

Passwork, which lets employees store company passwords in a digital vault, presents itself as a European firm based in Spain.

In reality, the researchers say, the software was developed in Russia in 2014, and a Russian sister company is certified by the FSB security service – a process requiring source code to be reviewed by state-approved auditors.

Novar, one of the largest builders of solar parks in the Netherlands, stopped using the software within a day of being informed by Investico and reported the matter to the National Cyber Security Centre. Regional broadcaster RTV Noord said it would carry on using it.

The journalists found no evidence that passwords were stolen or data compromised. Passwork’s chief executive denied any relationship with the Russian firm, saying the products merely share a common codebase origin.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Neurologist shortage threatens care for stroke and dementia
item.description:
Neurologist shortage threatens care for stroke and dementia

July 17, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The Netherlands has a growing shortage of neurologists, and hospital care for conditions such as dementia, stroke and Parkinson’s could come under strain as a result, the Dutch neurologists’ association has warned.

The population is ageing, which means more patients with age-related neurological conditions, and medical advances mean far more conditions can now be treated than in the past.

“That makes the work challenging, but all those treatment methods bring a greater demand for care with them,” association chairman Erwin Hoogervorst told broadcaster NOS.

At the same time, too few new neurologists are being trained to replace retirees.

The consequences are already visible, with one hospital having stopped taking on new neurology patients when its waiting list for a first appointment reached 1,200 people and waits of up to 18 months.

The number of training places is rising from 49 this year to 60 next year, but the six-year specialisation means the extra doctors will not reach hospitals until 2032.
The Dutch care sector is forecast to be 266,000 workers short by 2035.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: ASML staff get €20,000 in shares if they stay until 2030
item.description:
ASML staff get €20,000 in shares if they stay until 2030

July 17, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

ASML staff will be given a one-off package of shares worth €20,000 each, on condition they stay with the chip machine maker until at least the start of 2030.

The Veldhoven-based company informed its roughly 44,500 employees worldwide by email, according to the Eindhovens Dagblad. Staff will not be able to sell the shares before January 1 2030, and other conditions are still being worked out.

The package is a token of appreciation for staff efforts, “but above all for the work that will be needed in the coming years”, a spokesperson told broadcaster NOS.

The gesture comes days after ASML raised its full-year forecast for the second time this year, reporting second-quarter profits of €2.9 billion on sales of €9.3 billion as AI-driven demand continues to outpace expectations.

It also follows a turbulent start to the year for the workforce. In January, ASML said it would cut 1,700 jobs, mainly in the Netherlands, despite record 2025 results. In June, the company agreed with unions that there would be no forced redundancies until at least May 2027.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton
